% Claim proof file for row claim_3_21 -- "UnblockableNonCollidersOpen".

%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_21 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: UnblockableNonCollidersOpen
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_21}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_21}

\begin{Note}[UnblockableNonCollidersOpen]
%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
Note that unblockable non-colliders are always $C$-$\sigma$-open, regardless of the subset $C \ins V \cup J$.
\end{Note}


% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
Fix the walk $\pi = \lp v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n \rp$ in $G$ and a position $k$ for which $v_k$ is an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. We establish the two conjuncts of the claim in turn.

\emph{$v_k$ is not a collider on $\pi$.}
By \refrow{def_3_16}, $v_k$ being an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ requires in particular that $k$ is an interior position ($0 < k < n$) and that the joint $(v_{k-1}, v_k, v_{k+1})$ on $\pi$ falls into exactly one of the three sub-patterns
\[\begin{array}{rcl}
  \text{left chain:} & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}, \\
  \text{right chain:} & v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1}, \\
  \text{fork:} & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}.
\end{array}\]
Common to all three patterns is that the joint at $v_k$ never carries an arrowhead at $v_k$ on the edge $v_{k-1} \sus v_k$ \emph{and} an arrowhead at $v_k$ on the edge $v_k \sus v_{k+1}$ simultaneously: in the left chain only the right incident edge $v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ has an arrowhead at $v_k$, in the right chain only the left incident edge $v_{k-1} \suh v_k$ does, and in the fork neither does. Equivalently, the pattern $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ is excluded by the very definition of \refrow{def_3_16}. But by \refrow{def_3_15} that excluded pattern -- ``two arrowheads pointing at $v_k$'' -- is precisely the defining shape of a collider at $v_k$. Hence $v_k$ is not a collider on $\pi$.

\emph{$v_k$ is not a blockable non-collider on $\pi$.}
By the ``otherwise'' clause of \refrow{def_3_16}, a position $v_k$ on $\pi$ is by definition a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ exactly when $v_k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ \emph{and} $v_k$ is \emph{not} an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. The standing hypothesis -- that $v_k$ \emph{is} an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ -- directly negates the second conjunct of this characterisation, so $v_k$ is not a blockable non-collider on $\pi$.

The two predicates just refuted at $v_k$ are precisely the gating antecedents of \refrow{def_3_17}'s two $\sigma$-open universals (item~(1.i) ``every collider $v_k$ on $\pi$ lies in $\Anc^G(C)$'' and item~(1.ii) ``every blockable non-collider $v_k$ on $\pi$ lies outside $C$'') and of the two dual $\sigma$-blocking existentials (items~(2.i)--(2.ii)); since both refutations were established without any reference to the conditioning set, the position $v_k$ satisfies clause~(1) vacuously at $k$ and cannot be a witness of clause~(2) at $k$ regardless of the subset $C \ins V \cup J$, which is the asserted ``$C$-$\sigma$-openness regardless of $C$''.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Note — UnblockableNonCollidersOpen

Note that unblockable non-colliders are always $C$-$\sigma$-open, regardless of the subset $C \ins V \cup J$.

Proof.

Fix the walk $\pi = \lp v_0 \sus \cdots \sus v_n \rp$ in $G$ and a position $k$ for which $v_k$ is an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. We establish the two conjuncts of the claim in turn.

$v_k$ is not a collider on $\pi$. By def_3_16, $v_k$ being an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ requires in particular that $k$ is an interior position ($0 < k < n$) and that the joint $(v_{k-1}, v_k, v_{k+1})$ on $\pi$ falls into exactly one of the three sub-patterns \[\begin{array}{rcl} \text{left chain:} & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \hus v_{k+1}, \\ \text{right chain:} & v_{k-1} \suh v_k \tuh v_{k+1}, \\ \text{fork:} & v_{k-1} \hut v_k \tuh v_{k+1}. \end{array}\] Common to all three patterns is that the joint at $v_k$ never carries an arrowhead at $v_k$ on the edge $v_{k-1} \sus v_k$ and an arrowhead at $v_k$ on the edge $v_k \sus v_{k+1}$ simultaneously: in the left chain only the right incident edge $v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ has an arrowhead at $v_k$, in the right chain only the left incident edge $v_{k-1} \suh v_k$ does, and in the fork neither does. Equivalently, the pattern $v_{k-1} \suh v_k \hus v_{k+1}$ is excluded by the very definition of def_3_16. But by def_3_15 that excluded pattern – “two arrowheads pointing at $v_k$” – is precisely the defining shape of a collider at $v_k$. Hence $v_k$ is not a collider on $\pi$.

$v_k$ is not a blockable non-collider on $\pi$. By the “otherwise” clause of def_3_16, a position $v_k$ on $\pi$ is by definition a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ exactly when $v_k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ and $v_k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. The standing hypothesis – that $v_k$ is an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ – directly negates the second conjunct of this characterisation, so $v_k$ is not a blockable non-collider on $\pi$.

The two predicates just refuted at $v_k$ are precisely the gating antecedents of def_3_17's two $\sigma$-open universals (item (1.i) “every collider $v_k$ on $\pi$ lies in $\Anc^G(C)$” and item (1.ii) “every blockable non-collider $v_k$ on $\pi$ lies outside $C$”) and of the two dual $\sigma$-blocking existentials (items (2.i)–(2.ii)); since both refutations were established without any reference to the conditioning set, the position $v_k$ satisfies clause (1) vacuously at $k$ and cannot be a witness of clause (2) at $k$ regardless of the subset $C \ins V \cup J$, which is the asserted “$C$-$\sigma$-openness regardless of $C$”.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- claim_3_21 (`UnblockableNonCollidersOpen`): an *unblockable*
non-collider position on a walk `π` is *neither* a collider position
*nor* a blockable non-collider position. Consequently it is skipped
vacuously by both clauses of `IsSigmaOpen C` and cannot witness
either clause of `IsSigmaBlocked C`, *regardless of* the conditioning
set `C` -- which is the LN's "unblockable non-colliders are always
$C$-$\sigma$-open, regardless of $C \subseteq V \cup J$". -/
theorem not_isColliderAt_and_not_isBlockableNonColliderAt_of_isUnblockableNonColliderAt
    {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) {k : ℕ}
    (h : π.IsUnblockableNonColliderAt k) :
    ¬ π.IsColliderAt k ∧ ¬ π.IsBlockableNonColliderAt k :=
  ⟨(IsNonColliderAt_of_isUnblockableNonColliderAt π k h).2, fun hb => hb.2 h⟩
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem not_isColliderAt_and_not_isBlockableNonColliderAt_of_isUnblockableNonColliderAt
    {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) {k : ℕ}
    (h : π.IsUnblockableNonColliderAt k) :
    ¬ π.IsColliderAt k ∧ ¬ π.IsBlockableNonColliderAt k :=
  ⟨(IsNonColliderAt_of_isUnblockableNonColliderAt π k h).2, fun hb => hb.2 h⟩
lean_explanation:
In this theorem, `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type — the silent carrier the LN assumes whenever it talks about a graph $G$. The variable `G : CDMG α` is a *causal directed mixed graph* on `α` (declared in the surrounding `variable` block), and `π : Walk G v w` is a walk in $G$ from $v$ to $w$. The natural number `k` is a *position* on `π` — an index into the vertex sequence $v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_n$ — and is left implicit because the hypothesis `h : π.IsUnblockableNonColliderAt k` already pins it down at each call site. The braces around `{v w : α}` and `{k : ℕ}` are Lean's *implicit* binders: the elaborator infers these arguments from context, so consumers write `π.not_isColliderAt_and_not_isBlockableNonColliderAt_of_isUnblockableNonColliderAt h` rather than re-stating the endpoints and position by hand. The leading `π.` is Lean's *dot-projection* notation, which routes the call through the `Walk` namespace and treats `π` as the receiver.

The conclusion is a conjunction `¬ π.IsColliderAt k ∧ ¬ π.IsBlockableNonColliderAt k`. The first conjunct asserts that position $k$ is not a collider on $\pi$ — i.e. the joint at $v_k$ is *not* of the shape $v_{k-1} \to v_k \leftarrow v_{k+1}$ with two incoming arrowheads. The second asserts that $k$ is not a *blockable* non-collider — recall that on $\pi$ a non-collider position is partitioned into "unblockable" and "blockable" by `def_3_16`, with the blockable case defined as "non-collider *and* not unblockable".

The proof is a single anonymous-constructor term `⟨..., fun hb => hb.2 h⟩`. The first component extracts the `¬ π.IsColliderAt k` conjunct from the downstream helper `IsNonColliderAt_of_isUnblockableNonColliderAt`, whose returned `IsNonColliderAt` packages exactly the desired negation as its second projection. The second component is a one-line lambda that, given a hypothetical blockable-non-collider witness `hb`, projects out `hb.2 : ¬ IsUnblockableNonColliderAt k` and feeds it the standing `h` to derive `False`. No tactic block is needed — both halves dispatch in a single explicit term.
design_choices:
**Position-level conjunction, not a walk-level universal over $C$.** The LN's sentence — "unblockable non-colliders are always $C$-$\sigma$-open, regardless of $C$" — reads, on first glance, like a walk-level claim quantified over $C$. A careful reading shows it is per-*position*: at an unblockable non-collider position $k$, the gating predicates of $\sigma$-blocking's two clauses (`IsColliderAt k` and `IsBlockableNonColliderAt k`) both fail, and so the position contributes nothing to either side, *for any* $C$. Other positions on the same walk may still witness blocking or openness; the LN's "always" refers to the position, not the walk.

**The conjunction is $C$-free: "regardless of $C$" is a corollary of the statement's shape, not an enclosing quantifier.** The two conjuncts are exactly the negations of the gating antecedents of `IsSigmaOpen C`'s two universals. Discharging them at a position $k$ makes both universals vacuously satisfied at the $k$-th instance for any $C \subseteq V \cup J$, with no second proof step. So the LN's universal over $C$ collapses into a $C$-free statement instead of surviving as an outer quantifier we would have to carry around.

**One conjunctive theorem rather than two split lemmas.** The LN states a single observation, and every downstream consumer — claim 3.22's $\sigma$-separation symmetry pivot, claims 3.23 / 3.24's $\sigma$-separation equivalence rewrites, and chapter-4-onwards do-calculus reasoning — reaches for the conjunction as a unit. Mirroring the LN's single sentence as a single conjunctive theorem keeps the API ergonomic; consumers needing only one half extract it via `.1` / `.2`.

**Rejected alternative: walk-level $\forall C,\ \pi.\text{IsSigmaOpen}\ C$.** This transcription of "regardless of $C$" is too strong. It would require *no* colliders and *no* blockable non-colliders anywhere on the walk, which the LN does not claim — a walk with even one collider position falsifies the universal for a $C$ that excludes that collider from $\mathrm{Anc}^G(C)$, yet such a walk can still host unblockable non-collider positions to which the LN observation is meant to apply. The per-position reading is the only LN-faithful one.

**Rejected alternative: "every walk whose non-colliders are all unblockable is $C$-$\sigma$-open for all $C$".** This is also strictly stronger than the LN claim and conflates the per-position observation with a walk-level closure. The LN is talking about what unblockable positions contribute, not characterising walks built entirely from such positions.
